splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected by the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing plans to use more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release during absconding, and the judicially-created doctrine impermissibly extends punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any previously presented claim — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s separate requirements and are not subject to § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The split concerns whether candidates face a sufficiently concrete injury from election rules they believe are unlawful, or whether such challenges are too speculative before an election occurs.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury from allegedly unlawful election rules and have Article III standing to challenge them before the election takes place.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's alleged injury from a state election law is too speculative to confer Article III standing for a pre-election challenge.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency (or Sentencing Commission) interpretations of ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to agency/Commission interpretations when sentencing guidelines or criminal statutes are ambiguous. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while others defer to anti-defendant agency readings. The issue has gained urgency after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when a criminal statute or guideline is ambiguous, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in criminal sentencing contexts, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous provisions to prevail over lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses across the country.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) based on Application Note 1's commentary, or whether the text unambiguously excludes them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text is unambiguous and excludes inchoate offenses. This directly affects career-offender sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and that 'controlled substance offense' includes conspiracy and other inchoate offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Guideline text excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope beyond its text.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on such changes, while others permit courts to consider them as one factor among others. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state-controlled substance is not also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled. Some circuits hold that state-law offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling, while others require the substance to be on the federal schedule.

positions.label: State convictions qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include offenses under state law, with no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction is a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 348(f)(1)(A) of the Bankruptcy Code means that appreciation in property value occurring between the original filing and conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. This has significant consequences for debtors who convert in good faith but whose property has appreciated.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the longer civil appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (triggering the shorter criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case, and the criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or terms of their confinement (as opposed to fact or duration of confinement) exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether they may also use civil rights actions such as Bivens.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for all challenges relating to confinement, including conditions challenges, or whether civil rights actions remain available for claims not challenging the fact or duration of imprisonment. This affects federal prisoners' ability to challenge BOP decisions like denial of First Step Act time credits.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for confinement-related claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the terms of confinement, including earned time credits and prerelease custody, must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot be pursued through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is not the exclusive remedy for challenges to conditions or terms of confinement that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment, and civil rights actions remain available.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions when imposing a sentence upon revocation, or whether the revocation sentence must be limited to sanctioning the breach of the court's trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purpose of sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to punish defendants for their underlying violation conduct, placing it at odds with circuits that hold revocation sentences should only address the breach of judicial trust.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the underlying violation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for the conduct constituting the supervised release violation.

positions.label: Revocation sentences address breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be calibrated to the breach of the defendant's relationship with the court and the violation of the court's trust, not to punish the underlying conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denials of motions to seal medical records qualify for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of sufficiently 'high order.' Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of collateral orders, creating divergent approaches to immediate appealability.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' that warrants immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal, even those involving medical information, are not final orders subject to immediate appeal and must await final judgment in the underlying case.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of liability and available remedies under federal civil rights law?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve disagreement over the legal standards applicable to discrimination claims against business operators, suggesting the Second Circuit's approach conflicts with other circuits. The decision could impact hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that business operators face broader liability for discrimination claims with more expansive remedies available to plaintiffs alleging unfair treatment in hospitality or business settings.

positions.label: Narrower liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that claims of discrimination in business establishments are subject to narrower standards of liability and more limited remedies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker arranges transportation that results in injury. The issue affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's services.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not laws related to broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether legal errors or unfairness in a conviction — the type of claims typically raised in habeas petitions under Section 2255 — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration, while others permit it in certain circumstances.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider claims related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a substitute for habeas relief and courts may not consider legal challenges to the validity of a conviction as extraordinary and compelling reasons.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
